A 10 meter resolution global land cover product for the year 2021, developed and validated in near-real time based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data.  Data is provided with statistics for NUTS (Nomenclature of territorial units for statistics) boundaries.  Select the "statistics" tab and click on a feature to view.

sources.name: Waren Sea Normalised Difference Water Index, annual

meta.description: Normalised Difference Water Index.   The satellite images captured at low tide (bottom 30% of the springs tidal range) are used to compute a composite NDWI (Normalized Difference Water Index) image. The 70th percentile is taken when calculating the composite to give more weight to dry pixels.

This layer shows a time series of the Low Tide line from 2022 to 2025.  The contour was derived from all the low-tide cloud-free Landsat and Sentinel-2 images available.

For each section of contour, the data available in the layer comprises:
* *id*: unique identifier of the line
* *date*: date of the low tide event
* *springs_tide_range*: the mean springs tidal range at this location according to the FES2022 tidal model
* *obs_tide_level*: observed tide level across the satellite images used (average)
* *images_in_composite*: number of satellite images used to create the composite for this line

Select the data values tab and click on a feature to view details
Shows three separate COGs, hosted on the ESA PRR, that have been configured with a timestamp and an annual date picker UI.

The wind power density (w m 2) is a measure of the available wind resource at 100 meters height. Higher wind power density indicates greater wind power potential.  Constraints allow the data to be filtered by multiple criteria.

Global horizontal irradiation (kWh/m²/day), annual average. The greater amount of solar irradiation, the more electricity can be generated by a solar cell module. Selecting area with high solar potential contributes significantly to the desired efficiency and economic feasibility of a solar power project.

This collection contains representative datasets for the upcoming CHIME Copernicus Expansion Mission (CEM) generated as part of the Sentinel Users Preparation (SUP1) Rail-Adjacent Vegetation Monitoring with Copernicus Expansions (RAVE) project. The collection contains data for 3 AOIs across the UK.

The **True Color** bands are interpolated between 2% and 98% centiles.

### Spatial and Temporal Coverage
* Spatial coverage: Europe (UK)
* Temporal coverage: 2020/04/22–2025/04/17

### Further information
* [RAVE Home](https://rave.apex.esa.int/en)

Monthly maps of NOx shipping emissions across European waters at 0.5° spatial resolution, derived from regional atmospheric inversions. These posterior estimates assimilate TROPOMI NO₂ observations to refine the CAMS-REG-v8/STEAM shipping inventory, capturing monthly variations in maritime emission patterns.

This dataset provides gridded binary data at an hourly temporal resolution and at a finer spatial resolution of 0.05° × 0.1° from 2019 to 2023, covering the European maritime domain (15.25°W to 40°E; 25°N to 74.25°N).    In principle, STEAM produces detailed ship emission estimates by integrating vessel activity data from the Orbcomm Ltd AIS archive with technical specifications from the S&P Global databases. It models ship fuel consumption and emissions by incorporating its internal database of ship-specific parameters, such as engine load and abatement technologies, and environmental influences, including wind, sea ice, and ocean currents from the Copernicus Climate Data Store (CDS) and Copernicus Marine Environmental Monitoring Service (CMEMS). Recent refinements of STEAM include updated emission factors for NOx, CO2, SOx, PM2.5, and alternative fuels like methanol and ammonia, ensuring alignment with evolving maritime regulations and technologies.

sources.name: Maritime NO2 individual plumes, moving AOI

meta.description: Average NOx emission rate along the entire ship trajectory, from the STEAM model.

attribution.text: Laboratory for Climate and Environmental Sciences. Contact: hugo.vignesoult@lsce.ipsl.fr

meta.description:
- NOx (January 2019 – December 2024, kt NO₂-eq/month): Maritime NOx emissions are estimated using a bottom-up inventory (CAMS-REG-v8 prior) built from shipping activity data such as vessel traffic, engine types and fuel consumption, alongside a top-down inversion (posterior) that uses satellite observations and atmospheric transport modelling to estimate actual emissions. The two approaches show different seasonal cycles and diverging multi-year trends, indicating that activity-based records alone do not fully capture real-world shipping NOx behaviour.
- CH4 (January 2019 – December 2021, kt CH4/month): Methane emissions from sea shipping are estimated using the CAMS-REG-v8 prior inventory and a posterior derived from inversions. The prior and posterior estimates are very similar, suggesting that existing activity data reliably characterises maritime methane emissions without significant correction from satellite-constrained methods.
- FFCO2 (January 2019 – December 2024, kt CO₂/month): Fossil fuel CO₂ emissions from sea shipping are estimated from the CAMS-REG-v8 inventory and also derived indirectly from the NOx inversions, using known relationships between NOx and CO₂ in combustion processes. As with NOx, the inventory and inversion-derived estimates show different seasonal cycles and diverging trends over the six years, reinforcing that satellite-constrained approaches reveal emission patterns not captured by activity data alone.

**Instruction**
- Click on Data Values
- Click on any marine regions
- Click on Charts

attribution.text: Science Partners and LSCE, Contact audrey.fortems@science-partners.com and gregoire.broquet@lsce.ipsl.fr
